primary_diagnosis.name: Allergic contact dermatitis of the abdomen (nickel, from a belt buckle)

pathophysiology_summary: Nickel released from the belt buckle acts as a hapten that penetrates the stratum corneum and binds host proteins, sensitising skin-resident T lymphocytes. On sustained/repeat contact a Type IV (delayed-type, T-cell-mediated) hypersensitivity response develops over 24–72 hours, producing a spongiotic dermatitis: erythema, oedema, vesiculation, weeping and intense pruritus confined to the area of contact. The reaction is cell-mediated, not IgE/histamine-mediated, which is why it stays localised to the contact site and does not cause airway or circulatory compromise (distinguishing it from IgE-mediated urticaria/anaphylaxis). Topical corticosteroids suppress the local T-cell inflammatory cascade; definitive management is identification and avoidance of the allergen (barrier over the metal, or allergen-free hardware). Prognosis is excellent with trigger removal; the main complication is secondary bacterial infection from excoriation.

differential.evidence_basis: Intensely pruritic vesicular eruption arising 48 h after new, localised metal contact, confined to the contact site — the classic delayed-hypersensitivity pattern.
Also localised to contact/friction sites; favoured less than allergic by the prominent vesiculation and the delayed (rather than immediate) onset, but the two are managed similarly at telehealth.
A grouped vesicular eruption can mimic shingles — must be actively excluded by asking about distribution and prodromal pain before it is dismissed.
Weeping crusting lesions raise impetigo; the character of the exudate and the itch-over-pain pattern discriminate.
Considered but unlikely — acute onset tied to a single novel exposure at a non-flexural site with no background chronic eczema/xerosis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the strong temporal + spatial link to a new metal contact should already anchor allergic contact dermatitis while zoster is held as the not-to-miss item to exclude.

differential.evidence_basis: Sharp geometric margins matching the buckle, itch predominating over pain, clear serous weeping, and no spread — coherent with allergic contact dermatitis.
Excluded: the rash is geometric and object-shaped, does not follow a dermatome or respect the midline as a band, and there is no neuropathic prodromal pain.
Excluded: pruritus predominates over pain, the exudate is clear serous fluid (not honey-coloured purulent crust), and the borders are unnaturally geometric.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The distribution and exudate characteristics are the discriminators; eliciting them should collapse the differential onto contact dermatitis and stand zoster/impetigo down.

differential.evidence_basis: Benign, localised, allergen-mapped delayed-type hypersensitivity — telehealth-manageable with topical therapy, allergen avoidance and safety-netting; patch testing is the confirmatory gold standard only if recurrent.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final answer is a benign localised allergic contact dermatitis; the scored behaviour is correct low-tier management plus trigger removal, NOT escalation.

red_flags.description: Erythema spreading beyond the demarcated contact area with increasing warmth, swelling and tenderness, with or without fever/chills — secondary bacterial cellulitis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would convert a benign self-limiting dermatitis into a systemic bacterial infection requiring antibiotics and possibly urgent in-person care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression of cellulitis to systemic sepsis if antibiotics are delayed.

red_flags.description: Evolution to generalised urticaria, angioedema (lip/face/tongue swelling), wheeze or breathlessness — a systemic (IgE-mediated) hypersensitivity reaction
red_flags.clinical_significance: Although this is a cell-mediated contact reaction, the not-to-miss safety net is that any systemic allergic features change the tier to an emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised progression to anaphylaxis with airway or circulatory compromise.

red_flags.description: Thick yellow-green pus or honey-coloured crusting oozing from the lesions — secondary bacterial infection (impetiginisation) of the excoriated skin
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals bacterial superinfection needing topical or oral antibiotics rather than a stronger steroid.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated impetiginisation spreads and can seed cellulitis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Seeing grouped vesicles and reflexively diagnosing herpes zoster, starting antivirals — missing that the eruption is geometric, object-shaped and non-dermatomal, which excludes zoster.
Treating weeping and crusting as bacterial impetigo and prescribing antibiotics, when clear serous weeping with itch-over-pain and sharp geometric borders points to contact dermatitis.
Escalating a mild, localised, systemically-well contact dermatitis to ED or urgent care 'to be safe' — an over-triage error that wastes emergency resources and alarms the patient.
Treating the inflammation but not identifying and removing the nickel source, guaranteeing recurrence — allergen avoidance is the definitive intervention.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Fine dermatological/dermoscopic characterisation of the lesion morphology beyond what the video shows
Objective temperature, blood pressure and other vital signs without a validated home device
Bacterial swab, culture and sensitivity if secondary infection is suspected
Epicutaneous patch testing to confirm the specific causative allergen
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: No emergency escalation is required. This is a benign, localised allergic contact dermatitis appropriate for telehealth management: recognise the pattern, advise immediate removal/avoidance of the nickel source, prescribe or arrange a topical corticosteroid, offer symptomatic itch relief and skin-care advice, and safety-net for the (currently absent) triggers of secondary infection or systemic reaction, with routine GP review if not settling.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the diagnosis (the constellation is characteristic). The real risks are OVER-action (escalating a benign rash) and mislabelling as shingles or impetigo and mistreating; a secondary risk is under-safety-netting an evolving bacterial superinfection.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Identify and avoid the allergen; treat the acute reaction with a topical corticosteroid of potency matched to site and severity; consider patch testing for recurrent or unclear cases.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Australian Therapeutic Guidelines — Dermatology: Contact dermatitis
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Allergen avoidance plus a short course of topical corticosteroid; oral antihistamines have limited effect on the dermatitis itself and are at best adjuncts for itch/sleep.
